description: 38M low-speed MVC with neck pain, alert, sober — NEXUS candidate
transcript:
Dr. Foster: Tell me what happened.
Patient: I got rear-ended at a stoplight. The other car was going maybe twenty. My head snapped back.
Dr. Foster: Air bags deploy?
Patient: No. The car has minor damage.
Dr. Foster: Any loss of consciousness?
Patient: No.
Dr. Foster: How are you feeling now? Where does it hurt?
Patient: My neck is sore, mostly in the back. A little stiff. No headache really.
Dr. Foster: Any numbness, tingling, weakness anywhere?
Patient: No.
Dr. Foster: Any other injuries? Anywhere else hurt?
Patient: No, just the neck.
Dr. Foster: Any drugs, alcohol today?
Patient: No, I was on my way to work.
Dr. Foster: How old are you, any medical problems?
Patient: Thirty-eight. Healthy.
Dr. Foster: Alert, oriented x3, conversing normally. Let me palpate the cervical spine. Any tenderness here, midline? Here? Here?
Patient: A little tender right in the middle, lower part.
Dr. Foster: Midline at C6 area, mild tenderness. No step-off. Paraspinal muscles also tender bilaterally. Strength 5/5 throughout, sensation intact, reflexes symmetric.
Dr. Foster: He has midline tenderness so we can't clear him by exam alone. Let me think about what imaging he needs.

groundTruth.notes: Classic NEXUS application. Blunt trauma, doctor walking through the criteria explicitly. He has midline tenderness so the rule actually fails here — but that is exactly the value of surfacing it: it documents the failed criterion and justifies imaging. NEXUS supports the affirmative imaging decision as well as the rule-out.
